_meta.author: cowork-seed
_meta.review_notes: Seed example — the canonical lesson used as the blueprint §5.4 illustrative input/output pair.

output.objective: Solve pairs of linear equations using the elimination method.

segments.narration: Today we are going to learn how to solve simultaneous equations using elimination.
segments.subtitle: Simultaneous Equations: Elimination Method

visual.title: Simultaneous Equations
visual.subtitle: The Elimination Method
visual.objective_text: Solve pairs of equations by eliminating one variable.

segments.narration: Add the two equations. The y-terms cancel, leaving a single equation in x.
segments.subtitle: Add to eliminate y

segments.narration: Substitute x equals three back into the first equation to find y.
segments.subtitle: Substitute back to find y

segments.narration: Graphing both lines shows they cross at the point three, one — the same answer.
segments.subtitle: The lines meet at (3, 1)

segments.narration: A common mistake is subtracting the equations without checking signs. Line them up and watch the signs.
segments.subtitle: Watch the signs when you subtract

mistaken_approach.statement: Subtracting pair-wise without flipping signs correctly.
mistaken_approach.example: (2x + y) - (x - y) treated as (2x + y - x - y)

correct_approach.statement: Distribute the minus sign through every term.

visual.why_students_make_it: They forget the minus sign applies to every term inside the brackets.

segments.narration: Recap: align, add or subtract to eliminate, solve, substitute back, check.
segments.subtitle: Align → eliminate → solve → check

key_points: Add or subtract to eliminate one variable.
Substitute back into either original equation.
Check the answer in both equations.
visual.closing_statement: You can solve simultaneous equations by elimination.

branch_templates.example_question: Why did you add the equations?
branch_templates.branch_narration_template: Good question. We add the equations to eliminate the y-terms because y and minus y sum to zero.
Simultaneous means both equations are true at the same time — we want a single (x, y) pair that works for both.
